text: Hello everyone, this is Dr. Madhya Leba. This is the second patient that was going to show up to my live connect surgery. Unfortunately, this patient cancelled. This is the subsequent video.
This patient has a symptomatic incisional ventral hernia with incarceration of bowel and omentum. She's had a prior C-section which is likely the cause of her problems.

This can be quite difficult as the inferior portion of the lower abdominal area is very weak and the fascia in this area can be difficult to deal with. Most times there's actually no fascia, there's just muscle.

As per my routine, I like to go in triptomally, evaluate the hernia on this side, what type of repair I'm going to do. In this case, I wasn't sure if I was going to be able to do an ETEP or if I had to do a formal TAR or IPOM.
The surgeon performs a lateral docking on the left side, followed by a crossover maneuver, and then repositions the surgical ports for a top-down approach to address the hernia.
The surgeon performs a crossover dissection, staying high in relatively normal tissue, and completes a contralateral dissection before readjusting the ports for a top-down approach.

The surgeon begins a top-down approach by dissecting the tissue on either side of the incisional ventral hernia, ensuring there is no bowel present within the hernia sac before proceeding with further dissection.

I knew the hernia was gonna be quite tedious in this area and I was probably gonna have to reconstruct the posterior components. In addition, I worry more about the anterior components with closure, especially down in these areas. Most of the time, there's just attenuated muscle and very little fibers of fascia to close.
Fortunately, I did get it closed, and so far, so good.

The surgeon mobilizes the posterior components, carefully handling the fragile peritoneum along the space of Retzius. Bilateral lower abdominal tars are performed to ensure the peritoneum is securely closed, providing good coverage for the suprapubic defect.

The surgeon anchors the sine core mesh to the pubis using several probing sutures, aiming to maintain mesh integrity and prevent parachuting due to the significant defect and unhealthy tissue in the region.

I did mention at the live surgery from Connect that I typically don't use drains. However, I did place drains in this case. It's a game time decision usually. I'm a big fan of fibrin glue. this will then just collapse that space and hopefully seal it. In my mind I think it at least minimizes the risk of seroma. I mostly placed the drains in this case as it was quite loosey and I just felt I needed a couple drains for a couple days.

I really like going back into the abdomen and evaluating the repair. First off, there's no mesh interactions, no mesh exposure, no mesh potential complications. I don't see any bowel injury, no peritoneal tears, and I document all of this in the chart.
